33370: Glioblastomas are very serious brain tumors that have different types of cells, including special cells called glioblastoma stem cells. These stem cells can make more of themselves and help the tumor grow. They work differently from other cells in the tumor. We found that a protein called A20, which helps cells live longer and controls something called NF-kappaB, is more common in these stem cells compared to regular tumor cells. To see if A20 is important for the stem cells, we used a special tool to reduce its amount in the stem cells. When we did this, the stem cells grew and lived less well. This happened because they couldn't go through their normal cycle as much and had less protection against being damaged. The stem cells also became more sensitive to a substance called TNFalpha, which can cause them to die. Fewer stem cells meant the tumor had a harder time making new cells, especially when grown in lab dishes. Mice with human tumor cells that had less A20 lived longer. Looking at information from people with glioblastomas showed that those with more A20 tend to have better outcomes. Targeting A20 could be a way to fight these deadly brain tumors. Researchers used a special imaging technique to watch kidney cells called podocytes and another type of cell called parietal epithelial cells (PECs) move around inside a living mouse. They developed new mice that had special glowing tags to help them see these cells better. When they looked at the cells, they saw that podocytes sometimes came together in small groups and moved into a part of the kidney called Bowman's capsule. They also noticed that multiple podocytes moved at the same time. In another group of mice, they observed PECs moving towards podocytes and forming tiny connections between them. This shows that the kidney is more active and changing than previously thought. This new way of watching kidney cells could help scientists learn more about how the kidneys get hurt and heal.

Memory and how cells decide their fate Even cells that are exactly the same and live in the same place can look and act very differently. It's not always clear why they do this—whether it's just luck, outside influences, or the cells making their own choices. To understand this better, we watched thousands of tiny living things called Bacillus subtilis for many, many generations without changing anything. We noticed that some of these cells moved around freely, while others stuck together in chains. The cells that moved didn't seem to remember how long they had been moving or try to control it. But the cells that stayed in chains did a good job of sticking together for a set amount of time. This shows that the cells have different ways of deciding what to do. We found out that there are three special parts inside the cells that help them make these decisions. These parts work separately, so one part can start the process of sticking together, while another part keeps it going. When something outside the cell starts this process, it can lead to the cells forming a protective group called a biofilm. Our findings suggest that the cells can try out being part of a group on their own, and outside influences can make them stick together longer if needed.

Cold exposure makes heart problems worse by making cholesterol particles smaller and causing more fat to build up in arteries. This happens even if you don't eat more when it's cold. In special mice that can't handle fats well, staying cold for a long time leads to bigger, unstable patches forming inside their arteries. These patches are more likely to break off and cause heart attacks or strokes. A protein called UCP1, which helps keep some mice warm, stops this from happening. When UCP1 is missing, the mice get more artery problems. Also, being cold makes less of a hormone called adiponectin, and giving this hormone to mice helps prevent these artery issues. This study shows why cold weather might be dangerous for people's hearts.

Neutrophil extracellular traps, which are like webs made by white blood cells, often contain damaged pieces of DNA from inside the cells. These traps can cause the body to attack itself, similar to what happens in lupus. We don't fully understand how these traps are created or how they lead to inflammation without an infection. When certain parts of the cell called ribonucleoprotein immune complexes (RNP ICs) trigger the creation of these traps, the cell's powerhouses, known as mitochondria, become less active and move to the cell's outer layer. The damaged DNA from the mitochondria can be released outside the cell and causes inflammation. When this damaged DNA is put into mice, it triggers a response that leads to the production of a chemical called interferon, which is part of the body's defense system. People with a condition called systemic lupus erythematosus and those with another condition called chronic granulomatous disease, which affects the cell's ability to fight infections, also show signs of this damaged DNA being released and causing inflammation. Blocking the chemicals made by the mitochondria in mice helps reduce symptoms and interferon levels in a model of lupus. Overall, these results suggest that mitochondria play a key role in making both the traps and the damaged DNA that can lead to autoimmune diseases like lupus.

Restoring a process that helps remove old or damaged proteins in older liver improves how well the liver works. This process, called chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA), is like a trash disposal system inside cells that gets less effective as we get older. We found that this system stops working well in older animals, leading to more damaged proteins and problems in the body. In this study, we tried to keep this trash disposal system working well even as the animals got older. We created special mice that could control how much of the key part of this system was present. By keeping this key part active, we saw that the cells had fewer damaged proteins, were better at dealing with protein damage, and the liver worked better. This suggests that keeping this important cleanup process working could help prevent some of the problems that come with getting older.

Understanding how organisms change to better survive in certain conditions is a big question in science. It's hard to figure out exactly what parts of an organism change because many different parts might be involved, and each part could play a small role. We created a way to look at both experiments and computer models together to solve this problem. We used this method to learn how E. coli bacteria can tolerate more ethanol. We tested how changing one part of the bacteria at a time affects its ability to handle ethanol. Then, using computers, we looked at how all these changes fit together to form bigger processes inside the bacteria, like how they control water balance or build their outer shell. Surprisingly, we found that a key way the bacteria adapt is by changing how they process and use ethanol inside them. By studying the bacteria that can handle more ethanol after being grown in the lab, we learned about natural ways bacteria can become more tolerant to ethanol. These bacteria mostly followed similar paths to adapt as we predicted by looking at the overall picture of what helps them survive.

In people with celiac disease, their body makes special antibodies against a protein called transglutaminase 2 (TG2) when they eat foods containing gluten. These people have a certain gene that makes them more likely to get celiac disease. We studied the antibodies in their small intestine to understand how they form. We found that there are many more antibodies against TG2 in the small intestine of people with active celiac disease compared to healthy people. These antibodies stick strongly to TG2 but don't change much after being made. Unlike antibodies made during infections, these antibodies haven't grown quickly recently and won't last long outside the body. This suggests that the body has a ready-made set of antibodies that can quickly react to TG2. When these antibodies are made as different types, some stick to TG2 better than others. This might explain why the body responds so strongly to TG2 in celiac disease.

1568684: The special kind of fat called brown fat in people can help burn extra calories. Scientists found out that a substance called chenodeoxycholic acid, which is part of the digestive juice bile, can make brown fat more active. They gave 12 women this substance for two days and saw their brown fat working harder. This made them burn more energy overall. When they tested it in the lab on actual pieces of brown fat from people, they found that it made the fat cells use energy faster and make more of a special protein. This didn't happen when they tested it on regular white fat cells. This shows that bile acids might be a way to make brown fat work better in people.

In 1990 and 2015, we looked at how many young kids around the world were very thin. The United Nations wanted to cut the number of very thin kids in half by 2015. We studied how being very thin changed over time in different parts of the world. We used information from about 31 million children under 5 years old who took part in health surveys in 139 countries from 1965 to 2002. We found that worldwide, the number of very thin kids went down from 26.5% in 1990 to 17.6% in 2015. In richer countries, it dropped from 1.6% to 0.9%. In poorer areas, it fell from 30.2% to 19.3%. The overall goal was to see if progress was made in reducing very thin kids over those years.

1834762: Commensal and disease-causing bacteria living in our bodies can affect our weight, risk of cancer, and autoimmune conditions like lupus, even though we don't fully understand how. We discovered that when these bacteria form communities called biofilms, they create structures made from proteins and DNA. Specifically, we found that a protein called curli, which is part of these biofilm structures, combines with DNA to form strong fibers. This combination speeds up the process of protein clumping together and creates complex structures that can activate parts of our immune system. Certain immune cells, like dendritic cells, become more active and release chemicals called cytokines, some of which can be harmful in lupus. When we gave these fiber-DNA structures to mice, it caused their immune systems to react and produce antibodies against their own body parts, similar to what happens in lupus. We also saw that when lupus-prone mice were infected with bacteria that make curli, they produced more of these harmful antibodies than when infected with bacteria that don't make curli. This research suggests that the types of bacteria in our gut and their ability to form biofilms might play a role in causing or worsening autoimmune diseases like lupus, and could help us find new ways to treat these conditions. A study using a special test found some new parts of a signaling system that helps control how much an organ grows and keeps stem cells active. This system is called the Hippo-YAP pathway. We used a method that looks at many different types of cell signals to find these new parts. We discovered that a protein called LKB1, which usually stops tumors from growing, works by changing other proteins in the system. LKB1 affects where another protein called Scribble is found in the cell and how well a key part of the Hippo-YAP system works. We also learned that the protein YAP is important for LKB1 to do its job of stopping tumors. Our findings show how LKB1 and YAP are connected and could help in treating cancers caused by changes in LKB1. They also give us clues about what starts the Hippo-YAP system working.

Oncogenic signals from cancer cells attract special immune cells called myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs) to help start tumors. These immune cells play a big part in both the main tumor and spreading to other parts of the body. Doctors don't fully understand why some people with the same kind of cancer have different levels of these immune cells. By looking at human cancer samples and using mice with similar types of tumors, researchers found that when cancer cells have strong signals from something called mTOR, it makes the tumor attract more of these special immune cells. They also found that blocking these signals or things that make them stronger can slow down tumor growth. But if you give back these immune cells or a substance they produce, the tumor growth can come back. The cancer cells that start tumors make more of this substance. These immune cells then make more of the starting cells in the tumor, creating a cycle. Looking at real breast cancer samples and growing them in mice showed that these ideas work in people too. This study shows that mTOR signals help cancer attract harmful immune cells and explains how certain groups of cancer cells might change to need a specific mix of immune cells to grow.

LDL cholesterol levels: a big search for genes
BACKGROUND: High LDL cholesterol is linked to heart problems. Learning more about how our bodies handle and control LDL cholesterol could help find new ways to treat it.
We did a big search to find genes related to LDL cholesterol levels.
METHODS: We looked at blood samples from over 11,000 people to measure their LDL cholesterol. We checked more than 290,000 spots on their DNA that are common enough to study. We also used data from another set of tests on around 4,000 people to look at more than 290,000 spots. Then, we tested these findings in two other groups of people to make sure they were right.
We used special math tricks to find the most important spots and combined all the results to see how each spot affects LDL cholesterol levels.
FINDINGS: We found two spots in one part of the DNA that seem to be strongly linked to LDL cholesterol levels. Another test found a third spot that was also linked. These spots might give us clues about how to better understand and treat high LDL cholesterol.

Protein that gets added or removed with acetic acid can change how it works, and this is part of how our bodies control things. This process, called acetylation, happens when acetic acid is moved from one place to another on certain proteins. Special enzymes called acetyltransferases and deacetylases control this process. Changes in acetylation can affect how our body uses food, makes energy, and grows new blood vessels. When these changes go wrong, it can lead to problems like being overweight, having diabetes, or heart issues. We call these kinds of problems "metabolic complications." As we learn more about which proteins get acetylated, we're finding out that this process also affects how our immune system works and can play a role in diseases like Alzheimer's and Huntington's. This review looks at what we know about how acetylation helps control metabolism and leads to diseases related to how our body uses food and energy.

Local making and breaking down of proteins control how the tip of a growing nerve changes shape in response to signals. Only certain proteins are made right at the tip, while others travel from the cell body. It's not clear why just some proteins are made locally. Now, we've found that when the tip of a growing nerve gets a signal, it starts breaking down specific proteins, like RhoA, which helps the nerve change shape. If we stop the breakdown of RhoA, the nerve doesn't need to make new proteins to change shape. We also found that the main proteins broken down at the tip are those made there. So, breaking down proteins at the tip is important and makes sure the nerve can respond properly to signals by needing to make new proteins locally.

In human tuberculosis, there are areas called granulomas which are a sign of the disease. Doctors don't fully understand how they form or what they do. To learn more about granulomas, we looked at the proteins inside them without any preconceived ideas. We used special tools to carefully cut out parts of the granulomas, then studied them using powerful machines to see what proteins were present. We also used a microscope to take detailed pictures. We noticed that the center of the granulomas had a lot of things that make inflammation happen, like substances that fight infections and chemicals that can cause swelling. But the area around a part called caseum was less likely to cause inflammation. This pattern was seen in six people and some rabbits too. Even though the whole body has a mix of things that can either fight or cause inflammation, in each granuloma, these things are kept separate from each other. Based on our findings, we think that the way tuberculosis makes you sick might depend on where different parts of the inflammatory response are located as the granuloma forms.

In plants, cells develop specific boundaries and communicate with each other, which is essential for growing into complex structures. Special proteins called PINs help move a signaling chemical named auxin around the plant, guiding how different parts grow and bend towards light or gravity. Scientists wanted to understand how these PIN proteins become arranged in a specific pattern on the cell surface. They found that after being made inside the cell, PINs are first sent to the outside in a random way. Then, they move back inside and get sorted to the right places on the cell surface. When this process is stopped, it can cause problems in how auxin moves around, leading to unusual growth patterns where leaves might start to look like roots. This suggests there are two main steps in setting up the correct arrangement of PIN proteins on the cell surface.

Half the world's population has a chronic infection with a bacteria called Helicobacter pylori, which can cause stomach inflammation, ulcers, and an increased risk of stomach cancer. This bacteria needs a special door, called HpUreI, to let in a chemical called urea when the stomach is very acidic. When the stomach is acidic, this door opens quickly to let urea in so it can be used by an enzyme inside the bacteria. This enzyme, urease, turns urea into ammonia and carbon dioxide, which helps neutralize the acid in the stomach and keeps the area around the enzyme at a more neutral pH. In this study, we found out what this special door looks like. We discovered that the door is made up of six parts that fit together like a ring, with a fatty layer in the middle. Each part of the door has six long, twisty pieces that form the channel. These pieces create a unique shape that hasn't been seen before, with a special pattern of twists. Both the channel and how the parts fit together have important features that are similar to other related doors. Most of the channel is lined with parts that are either dark or light on the outside, and there are two narrow spots in the middle of the channel. Changing one specific spot, called Trp 153, in the middle part of the channel might affect how well the door works.

Somatic sex identity is cell-autonomous in the chicken In animals like mammals, embryos start as gender-neutral until a special gene tells them to develop into males or females. This idea is believed to work for all animals with backbones, but it hasn't been proven. We looked at three chickens that had both male and female parts on different sides of their bodies, which is very rare. This helped us understand how sex is determined in birds. Our findings showed that these chickens were actually made up of both male and female parts, suggesting that bird cells might already know if they are male or female. To test this idea, we moved some specific body parts from one type of embryo to another. For example, we took tissue from a female embryo and put it into a male embryo. In mammals, mixing these tissues usually causes problems, but in chickens, the cells from the female tissue kept their female characteristics and did what female parts do. Our research shows that bird cells already know if they are male or female, and that in birds, becoming male or female happens mostly because of the cells themselves.

Mosaic changes in a gene called PPM1D are linked to a higher risk of getting breast or ovarian cancer. New sequencing tools let us look at rare genetic differences that might play a role in common diseases. But it's tricky to design studies, analyze data, and make sure results are real. By studying 1,150 samples using advanced sequencing methods for 507 important genes involved in fixing damaged DNA, and then checking these findings in 13,642 people, we found that rare changes in the PPM1D gene are more common in people with breast or ovarian cancer compared to those without. Specifically, out of 7,781 cancer cases, 25 had these changes, while only 1 out of 5,861 healthy people did. Of the breast cancer cases, 18 had these changes, and in ovarian cancer cases, 12 did. These changes were found in both types of cancer but were mostly seen in a small part of the gene. When we tested how these changes affect the gene's function, we saw that they make the gene work extra hard to turn off p53, which is important for protecting cells from damage. So even though these changes stop the gene from making a full protein, they still seem to have a big effect on how the gene works. To sum up, mosaic changes in PPM1D can increase the risk of breast or ovarian cancer by making the gene overactive.

4423559: Planar cell polarity helps shape the embryo during a critical stage called neurulation. Sometimes, problems during this stage can cause issues like neural tube defects, which happen in about one out of every 1,000 babies. Studies on mice and frogs showed that a protein called Vangl2, which is part of a signaling system, might help guide cells to the center of the embryo. Now, researchers looked at zebrafish to see if this protein does the same thing. They found that Vangl2 helps align cells along the front-to-back direction of the embryo. During cell division, this alignment is briefly lost but then comes back as new cells join the tissue layer. When zebrafish lack Vangl2, their cells lose this alignment, struggle to rejoin the tissue layer, and form extra clusters of cells, leading to neural tube defects. Interestingly, stopping cell division can fix some of the problems caused by missing Vangl2, even though cells still don't move properly. This study shows how Vangl2 and its signaling system connect cell division with proper embryo shaping during neurulation, and suggests a new way that neural tube defects might develop. This study shows how Vangl2 and its signaling system connect cell division with proper embryo shaping during neurulation, and suggests a new way that neural tube defects might develop.

In the developing human body, special cells called cardiovascular progenitor cells grow into different types of heart cells. These include beating heart cells, blood vessel cells, and cells that make up the walls of blood vessels. Previous research on mice showed that all these heart-related cells come from a very early type of cell called a KDR-positive cardiovascular progenitor cell. To see if something similar happens in humans, scientists studied how human embryonic stem cells (cells that can become any type of cell) turn into heart cells. They found that when they grew these stem cells in special conditions, they produced a group of cells that could become heart cells, blood vessel cells, and cells that line blood vessels. When these cells were grown in flat layers, more than half turned into beating heart cells. This suggests there are similar early cells in humans that can grow into different types of heart cells.

BDNF and its receptor TrkB are important for how brain cells change over time, helping animals learn. It's not clear if BDNF is released and TrkB is activated during a specific type of cell change called structural long-term potentiation (sLTP). To find out, scientists used special tools to watch what happens inside tiny parts of brain cells called dendritic spines in mouse brain samples grown in the lab. They found that after sLTP was triggered, TrkB was quickly and continuously activated in the spines that were affected. This activation depended on certain signals and the production of BDNF in the spines themselves. The scientists confirmed that BDNF is present in the spines by looking at them under a powerful microscope. They also showed that BDNF can be released very quickly from individual spines when they are stimulated. These results suggest that BDNF and TrkB work together inside the spines to help with both the physical changes and the improved function of the brain cells. This discovery shows that each spine can make its own BDNF and use it to signal to itself, all controlled by specific brain signals.

The effect of cholesterol-lowering medicines (like statins) on the risk of broken hips in older people. RECENT BACKGROUND Some studies suggest that cholesterol-lowering medicines might help build stronger bones, but it's not clear if these medicines actually reduce the chance of breaking a bone in older people. GOAL To find out if taking cholesterol-lowering medicines lowers the risk of breaking a hip. STUDY TYPE We compared two groups of older people: those who had a broken hip and needed surgery, and those who didn't. We looked at how many people in each group took cholesterol-lowering medicines before their surgery. We also considered other factors like age, gender, and any other health issues they might have had. KEY FINDINGS People who took cholesterol-lowering medicines in the last six months (OR, 0.50; 95% CI, 0.33-0.76) or three years (OR, 0.57; 95% CI, 0.40-0.82) before their surgery were less likely to break a hip. This was true even when we considered other things that could affect their risk, like their race and whether they used other medicines.

In efforts to find new medicines for Alzheimer's disease, which has not worked well in people even though it seemed promising in animals, scientists used special brain cells made from people who carry a gene that makes them more likely to get Alzheimer's. These brain cells showed that the gene made them produce more of a protein linked to Alzheimer's and damaged certain types of brain cells. The gene also caused problems in human brain cells but not in mouse brain cells. By changing the gene back to its normal form, scientists could fix these problems, proving that the gene was causing the issues. When scientists introduced the problem-causing gene into normal brain cells, it caused similar damage, showing how the gene can make things worse. Treating the problem-causing brain cells with a special medicine helped improve their condition, suggesting that fixing the gene's shape might be a good way to treat Alzheimer's in people who carry this gene.

High-speed tests found many new genes important for turning a common bacteria, Bacillus subtilis, into a resting state. Scientists have known about many of these genes for a long time, but recent studies showed more genes needed for this process. We used a special technique to look for any missing genes. Folic acid helps blood vessels work better in people with narrowed heart arteries without much change in a certain chemical called homocysteine. BACKGROUND: Homocysteine might make heart arteries narrower, but it's not clear if reducing it can prevent heart problems. If reducing homocysteine could help, lowering it by 25% would be safe and cheap. This can be done with 0.4 mg of folic acid daily. Some say higher amounts of folic acid (5 mg daily) can improve how well blood vessels work, but the reason isn't clear. It's thought that this might happen by lowering total or free homocysteine. We wanted to see if folic acid could improve blood vessel health even before changing homocysteine levels in people with narrowed heart arteries. METHODS AND RESULTS: We gave 33 people with narrowed heart arteries 5 mg of folic acid daily for 6 weeks. We checked how well their blood vessels worked using a test called flow-mediated dilation (FMD) before giving them folic acid, 2 hours after the first dose, 4 hours after the first dose, and after 6 weeks. After just an hour, their blood had more folic acid (200 compared to 25.8 units). Blood vessel function got better 2 hours after taking folic acid (83 compared to 47 units) and was mostly back to normal by 4 hours (101 compared to 51 units). The amount of homocysteine didn't change much right away (after 4 hours, 9.56 compared to 9.79 units). Free homocysteine didn't change much at 3 hours but was a little lower later.

Understanding how the body fights malaria inside the body using the idea of "effective spread". The immune system and running out of red blood cells help control malaria levels, which can make the illness worse or better and spread more or less. Figuring out exactly how much each of these factors affects malaria over time is hard. Using information from mouse malaria, we calculated the "effective spread", which shows how different body defenses work together over time and how much the starting amount of malaria matters. We found that the body's first line of defense against malaria gets less effective as the amount of malaria increases. Also, making the body's defenses stronger can indirectly lower malaria levels by using up red blood cells. This way of looking at things helps us understand how the body controls malaria and could help in finding better treatments or vaccines for people.

5304891: In healthy people, our bodies react differently to germs like bacteria and fungi. We wanted to understand these differences and how genes might play a role. We studied 197 people from Europe who took part in a big health project. We looked at their blood cells when they were exposed to different types of germs over several years. We noticed that the body's reaction to each germ was unique, not just following one type of defense process. We also found that certain parts of people's DNA could affect how much of certain chemicals, called cytokines, their bodies made in response to these germs. Six specific areas in our DNA seemed to have a big impact on making a chemical called IL-6 when exposed to many different germs. One of these areas was linked to getting a serious fungal infection. The DNA changes we found were also connected to other health issues like infections and heart problems. This helps us understand why some people's immune systems react differently to germs.
5373138: In this study, we explore how human embryonic stem cells, which can become any type of cell, organize their genetic material in three dimensions. We focused on two types of these cells: naive and primed. Naive cells are like blank slates, while primed cells are more specialized but still flexible. To understand how these cells arrange their genes, we looked at special parts of the DNA called enhancers and insulators. Enhancers help turn genes on, and insulators keep genes separate from each other. We used a technique to see where proteins called cohesins, which help hold DNA together, are attached. This helped us create a map of how the DNA is folded and connected in these cells.

One idea is that when our body's defenses react to a germ, they sometimes also attack our own tissues because the germ looks similar to parts of our body. This has been suggested as a reason for conditions like diabetes, lupus, and multiple sclerosis. However, it's hard to prove which germs actually cause these autoimmune responses.

The Ly49Q receptor helps neutrophils move quickly to fight infections and inflammation. Normally, it keeps neutrophils from sticking too much to other cells. But when there's an infection or inflammation, it helps neutrophils change shape and move faster to reach the problem area. This happens through a special process involving different parts of the cell membrane. The Ly49Q receptor seems to control how these parts of the cell work, allowing neutrophils to switch from a resting state to an active fighting state when needed. It does this by managing how signals are sent within the cell and how certain molecules move around on the cell surface.

The effect of diabetes on how patients do over a long time after having unstable chest pain or a type of heart attack without certain signs on an ECG: Results from a big study. BACKGROUND: When people have trouble with their heart's blood flow, they often need to go to a special heart care unit. But we don't know how these people will do in the long run. It's especially important to understand this for people with diabetes because they often have more health problems after a heart attack. METHODS AND RESULTS: We looked at information from a large study called OASIS that followed people from six different countries who were in the hospital for unstable chest pain or a specific kind of heart attack. Out of all the people in the study, 21% had diabetes. People with diabetes were more likely to have a procedure to fix their heart arteries (23% vs. 20%), but they had similar rates of other heart procedures. Having diabetes made it more likely that someone would die (1.57 times more likely), have another heart attack, have a stroke, or develop heart failure. Women with diabetes were also more likely to have worse outcomes than men (1.98 times more likely for dying and 1.28 times more likely for heart issues).

In older people who have trouble sleeping every night, we compared two ways to help them get better rest: talking therapy and taking a pill. Many older folks have trouble sleeping, which can make them feel sick, lonely, and worried. Some studies say that both talking to a therapist and taking medicine can help, but no one has done a proper test where neither the doctors nor the patients know who gets the real treatment or a fake one. We wanted to see if talking therapy or taking a pill worked better for helping older people who couldn't sleep for a long time. We did a study with 46 older people who had trouble sleeping for a long time. Half got talking therapy, half took a sleeping pill, and half took a pretend pill. Everyone got their treatment for 6 weeks, and then we checked how well they slept after 6 more weeks. We looked at how much time they spent awake and asleep, how efficiently they slept, and how deeply they slept. R

ADAR1 works with Dicer to help process tiny pieces of RNA called microRNAs and control gene activity. In this research, we looked at how ADAR1's job of changing certain parts of RNA interacts with Dicer's role in breaking down RNA. We discovered that ADAR1 teams up with Dicer to speed up the cutting of microRNA pieces and helps put these pieces into special groups that silence genes. This shows a new way ADAR1 helps with microRNA processing and controlling gene activity. ADAR1 does different jobs in RNA editing and RNA control by forming either two ADAR1s working together or one Dicer and one ADAR1 working together. When there's no ADAR1 in mouse embryos, it stops many microRNAs from working, which changes how their target genes behave and may cause problems in the development of the embryo.

Embryos can naturally grow back and form normal body parts even if cut in half. How does this happen? We found that removing certain growth signals in frog embryos stops them from growing back normally. Specifically, turning off four key signals—ADMP and three types of BMP (BMP2, 4, and 7)—causes the embryo to grow all brain tissue everywhere instead of just where it should be. ADMP is turned on in a special area called the organizer when there are low levels of another signal called BMP. When the signals that usually stop ADMP (called BMP2/4/7) are removed from the bottom part of the embryo, ADMP starts working more, allowing the embryo to grow back properly. ADMP works like BMP and uses a similar way to send its message. But it can't work in the top part of the embryo because of a blocker called Chordin. Two other blockers, Sizzled and Bambi, help control where ADMP works. By moving pieces from the top or bottom of an embryo that doesn't have these signals, we showed that both ends can act as signal centers to grow back different types of tissue over long distances. This suggests that the signals from the top and bottom of the embryo, along with the blockers they face, help the embryo grow back correctly. To make it simple, imagine the embryo as a puzzle. The top and bottom each have special pieces that tell the rest of the puzzle how to fit together. When some pieces are missing, the embryo needs to use the remaining pieces to figure out how to complete the puzzle. In this case, the top and bottom pieces are giving instructions about which parts of the embryo should become what kind of tissue. By moving these pieces around, we can see how the embryo uses them to grow back properly. To sum up, the signals from the top and bottom of the embryo, along with the blockers they meet, create a system that helps the embryo grow back and form the right body parts.

The American Heart Association's guidelines for doctors about using special medicines called ACE inhibitors for people with heart and kidney problems. These medicines help lower blood pressure and can also help people with heart failure and damaged kidneys. While these medicines often improve blood flow to the kidneys and help the body get rid of extra salt, sometimes they can cause temporary trouble with how well the kidneys work. This problem, known as "functional kidney trouble," can happen right after starting the medicine or even after taking it for a long time. It's more likely to happen if the person already has low blood pressure or other issues that make it hard for the kidneys to get enough blood. The amount of waste the kidneys remove depends a lot on how much blood is flowing through them.
Renal considerations in angiotensin converting enzyme inhibitor therapy: a statement for healthcare professionals from the Council on the Kidney in Cardiovascular Disease and the Council for High Blood Pressure Research of the American Heart Association.

Feeding the fruit fly Drosophila a special medicine called rapamycin makes them live longer. This medicine works on a part of the body that helps sense nutrients. When this part doesn't work as much, it can make different kinds of animals, even humans, live longer. We found that giving adult fruit flies rapamycin made them live longer, just like some flies with a naturally weaker nutrient-sensing system. These flies also became better at surviving without food and dealing with a chemical called paraquat. By studying how this happens, we learned that rapamycin mainly affects a specific part of the nutrient-sensing system called TORC1. It does this by changing how cells break down old parts and make new proteins. Rapamycin can also help flies live longer even if their bodies are already good at living longer due to less food or a weaker insulin system. This suggests there are other ways rapamycin helps flies live longer.

Reproductive years and dementia risk in older women. Context: Some people think that taking hormones after menopause might help prevent memory problems. It’s also thought that having more reproductive years could be linked to a lower chance of getting memory issues, but this hasn’t been studied much. Goal: Find out if women who naturally go through menopause and have more reproductive years are less likely to get dementia or Alzheimer's disease. Method: We looked at data from a big study in the Netherlands where they followed thousands of women over time. Participants: Over 3,600 women aged 55 or older who didn’t have memory problems when the study started. They provided information about when they first got their periods, when they went through menopause, and what kind of menopause they experienced. Follow-up: Women were checked again in 1993-1994 and 1997-1999, and their health was tracked for any signs of memory problems. Main results: We found out how many women developed memory problems during the study and compared it based on how long they had reproductive years. Results: Over 21,000 years of following these women, 199 developed memory problems, including 159 who got Alzheimer's. After considering their age, we didn't see a clear link between longer reproductive years and a lower risk of memory problems.

Activating changes in a specific gene called NT5C2 make some leukemia cells resistant to chemotherapy in patients who have had the disease before. Leukemia is a type of cancer that starts in blood-forming tissue such as the bone marrow. Even with strong treatments, many children and adults don't get better or can get sick again. By looking at the entire DNA of these patients' cells, we found that 19 out of every 100 cases of a certain type of leukemia in children and 3 out of every 35 cases in adults had changes in the NT5C2 gene. The changes in this gene make the leukemia cells break down chemotherapy drugs faster, which helps them survive the treatment. When we put these changed genes into leukemia cells in the lab, the cells became more resistant to two common chemotherapy drugs. This suggests that these gene changes play a big part in why some people's leukemia gets worse or doesn't respond to treatment.

Fate mapping shows where and how different types of white blood cells, like monocytes and macrophages, start and change over time in healthy bodies. This makes them stop dying when they should and start to change in ways that can lead to cancer. Scribble also works with other cancer-causing genes to turn normal cells into cancer cells. In some cases, Scribble moves to the wrong place in the cells, which can also cause problems. Many breast tumors in both mice and people have less Scribble or have Scribble in the wrong place. This suggests that Scribble plays a key role in preventing breast cancer and that problems with its function can lead to abnormal cell growth and tumor formation.

In the body, when T cells get activated, they form tiny groups called microclusters where they start talking to each other using a special receptor. It's not clear if they keep talking in a bigger group in the middle called the central supramolecular activation cluster (cSMAC). We found that the cSMAC has more of a protein called CD45 and contains a special fat that helps break down old cell parts. When we tried to stop the T cells from talking by blocking their main communication channel, they stopped talking quickly. But the T cells kept forming microclusters for up to 10 minutes. This suggests that the T cells keep talking in these microclusters, but then stop in the cSMAC, where they might be sorted out for breakdown. Our research also shows that a part of the cell called F-actin plays a bigger role in T cell activation than just helping to make microclusters.

Although how well brain cells called neurons get sick from germs can greatly affect how people feel when they're sick, scientists don't know much about what makes some neurons more likely to get infected than others. In this study, we found that neurons from two different parts of the brain react differently to certain types of viruses. Neurons from the cerebellum and those from the cerebral cortex have special ways of fighting off germs that make them more or less likely to be infected. We also discovered that by giving neurons from one part of the brain extra help, we could make them better at fighting off viruses. This extra help came from three special genes that are usually active in another type of neuron. To investigate the long-term effects of radioactive iodine on thyroid function and size in patients with non-toxic multinodular goiter, we studied 69 consecutive patients who were selected for radioactive iodine treatment due to a growing goiter causing local compression symptoms or cosmetic issues. The treatment was chosen when surgery was risky, had been previously performed, or refused by the patient. Thyroid function tests and ultrasound-determined thyroid volume were measured before treatment and at 1, 2, 3, 6, and 12 months post-treatment, with annual follow-ups thereafter. Of the 69 patients, 56 received a single dose, 12 received two doses, and 1 received four doses. In 45 patients who remained euthyroid after a single dose, the median thyroid volume decreased from 73 (interquartile range 50-106) ml to 29 (23-48) ml at 24 months, as measured during follow-up. The median reduction was 40 (22-48) ml (60% reduction, p < 0.0001), with half occurring within three months. Patients treated with multiple doses or those who developed hypothyroidism or hyperthyroidism also experienced significant reductions in thyroid volume. To investigate how giving a special kind of iodine to people with a swollen thyroid gland that wasn’t causing problems could affect their health over time, we studied 69 people who had a growing, multi-part thyroid swelling that was pressing on other parts of their body or making them look different. These people didn’t want or couldn’t have surgery. We gave them the special iodine once, twice, or even four times. We checked their thyroid’s size and how it was working before giving them the iodine and then every month for a year and then yearly after that. After 2 years, most people who got just one dose of the iodine had their thyroid get much smaller. Half of the shrinkage happened in the first few months

But no one had checked if doing HPV tests twice, five years apart, would work well. We wanted to see if doing an HPV test during the first check-up would help find more severe changes or cancers during the second check-up, which happens five years later.

Epigenetic therapy helps fight ovarian cancer in mice by making the cancer less harmful to the immune system and shrinking the tumor. Ovarian cancer is one of the deadliest women's cancers, and we need better treatments. The cancer often makes it hard for the body's defenses to work against it. In experiments with mice, we found that using certain drugs to change how genes work can make the immune system stronger against the cancer. This happens when these drugs cause the body to produce more of a special signal called type I interferon. This signal helps increase the number of immune cells in the tumor area and makes them more active. It also reduces the number of cells that usually protect the tumor. Adding another type of drug to the first one makes the immune system even stronger and helps more cells fight the cancer. This could lead to better ways to treat ovarian cancer in people.
